title: Your Reputation Is Online: Managing Reviews Before They Manage You
subtitle: Every potential customer reads your reviews before they visit. What do they find?

toc.label: Why Reviews Are Your Most Powerful Marketing
Google Business Profile: The Basics Most Cafes Miss
Responding to Every Review — Yes, Every One

tools: T135-01 · Google Review QR Code Kit
T135-04 · Post-Visit Review Request Email Template

sections.heading: Why Reviews Are Your Most Powerful Marketing
sections.body:
A potential customer searches "coffee near me." Google shows three cafes. One has 4.7 stars and 380 reviews. Another has 4.2 stars and 45 reviews. The third has 3.8 stars and 12 reviews.

The first cafe wins most of that traffic. Not because it is the best cafe — but because it has the most social proof.

Online reviews are the modern version of word-of-mouth, except they are permanent, public, and searchable. They influence:
- **Whether someone visits for the first time** (87% of consumers read reviews for local businesses)
- **What they order** ("the banana bread is amazing" in a review drives sales of banana bread)
- **Whether they return** (a bad experience they voiced and you resolved can deepen loyalty)
- **Your Google ranking** (more reviews and higher ratings push you up in local search results)

Reviews are also free. You do not pay for a five-star Google review. You earn it by being good — and then by making it easy for happy customers to say so.

Most cafes have an asymmetry problem: unhappy customers leave reviews spontaneously. Happy customers need a gentle prompt. Your job is to close that gap.

sections.heading: Google Business Profile: The Basics Most Cafes Miss
sections.body:
Your Google Business Profile is often the first thing a potential customer sees. It shows up in Maps, in search results, and on phones. If it is incomplete or outdated, you are losing customers before they walk through the door.

The essentials to check and update quarterly:

**Opening hours.** Are they correct? Including holidays, seasonal changes, and any days you close early. Wrong hours on Google is one of the most common complaints in cafe reviews — and one of the easiest to fix.

**Photos.** Google prioritises listings with recent, high-quality photos. Upload 5–10 new photos every month: the interior, the counter, your best-looking drinks and food, the team. Avoid stock photos — customers can tell. Natural light, real products, real space.

**Menu.** Upload your current menu. If you change seasonally, update it. A customer who arrives expecting an item they saw online and finds it gone will be disappointed.

**Category.** Make sure your primary category is correct (Coffee Shop, Cafe, Bakery). Add secondary categories if relevant.

**Description.** Write 2–3 sentences about what makes your cafe unique. Not marketing jargon — just what a friend would say when recommending you.

**Attributes.** Wheelchair accessible? Dog-friendly? Outdoor seating? Free wifi? These filters matter to customers searching with specific needs.

**Posts.** Google lets you post updates, offers, and events. Use this weekly — it signals to Google that your listing is active, and it gives customers another reason to visit.

sections.heading: Generating Reviews Without Begging
sections.body:
Asking for reviews feels awkward, but there are natural, dignified ways to encourage them.

**The QR code method (T135-01).** Print a small card or sticker with a QR code that links directly to your Google review page. Place it:
- On the counter near the till
- On table tents
- On takeaway cup sleeves or bags
- On the receipt

The text should be simple: "Enjoyed your visit? Leave us a Google review" with the QR code. No pressure, just a prompt.

**The verbal ask.** Train staff (T135-02) to recognise moments when a customer is visibly happy — complimenting the coffee, thanking the barista, saying they will be back. That is the moment for: "That's great to hear — if you have a minute, a Google review would really help us out." Not every customer, not every time — just when the moment is right.

**The follow-up email (T135-04).** If you collect customer emails (through loyalty, wifi sign-up, or catering orders), send a brief follow-up 24 hours after their visit. "Thanks for visiting [cafe name] yesterday. If you enjoyed it, a quick Google review helps other people find us." Include a direct link.

**The post-catering ask.** After a corporate catering order or event, ask the organiser for a review. Business customers leave detailed, articulate reviews that carry weight.

What NOT to do:
- Do not offer discounts or freebies in exchange for reviews (Google penalises this)
- Do not ask for "five-star reviews" — ask for honest reviews
- Do not fake reviews or ask friends to write generic ones (customers can spot these)

sections.heading: Responding to Every Review — Yes, Every One
sections.body:
Responding to reviews is not optional. It signals to customers — current and future — that you are engaged, attentive, and human.

**Positive reviews (4–5 stars):**
Thank them by name. Reference something specific in their review. Keep it brief and warm.

"Thanks so much, Anna! Really glad you enjoyed the oat latte — it's one of our favourites too. See you next time."

Do NOT copy-paste the same response for every positive review. Vary your language. Mention specific items they praised. Three identical "Thank you for your kind words!" responses in a row look like a bot.

**Neutral reviews (3 stars):**
Thank them, acknowledge what they liked, and address what they did not. Show that you are listening and improving.

"Thanks for the feedback, Tomas. Glad the food hit the mark — sorry the wait was longer than usual. We've adjusted our peak staffing since then and things are moving faster. Hope you'll give us another try."

**Negative reviews (1–2 stars):**
See the detailed guidance in the "Turning Complaints Into Loyalty" article. In brief: respond quickly, acknowledge specifically, do not argue, take it offline.

Respond to every review within 48 hours. Set a calendar reminder or make it part of your weekly management rhythm.

sections.heading: Dealing With Unfair Reviews
sections.body:
Sometimes reviews are unfair. Factually wrong. Exaggerated. Occasionally malicious — from a competitor, a disgruntled ex-employee, or someone who visited a different cafe entirely.

Your response matters more than the review itself.

**Factual errors:** Correct politely. "We want to clarify — we do offer oat milk and have for over a year. We'd love to welcome you back and get your order right."

**Exaggerations:** Acknowledge the core complaint without validating the extreme. "We're sorry the wait was longer than expected during our Saturday rush. We're working on peak-hour staffing to reduce this."

**Fake or malicious reviews:** Google allows you to flag reviews that violate their policies (spam, fake, conflict of interest). Flag them, but do not wait for removal — respond publicly anyway. "We have no record of this visit and can't find this order in our system. We take feedback seriously and would love to hear from you directly at [email] so we can investigate."

**The angry regular:** Sometimes a loyal customer has a bad day and leaves a disproportionately harsh review. Reach out privately first — a phone call or in-person conversation usually resolves it, and many will update or remove the review voluntarily.

Never:
- Get into a public argument
- Accuse the reviewer of lying (even if they are)
- Threaten legal action in a review response
- Get personal or emotional

Every response is a public performance. Your audience is not the reviewer — it is everyone else reading.

sections.heading: Building a Weekly Review Rhythm
sections.body:
Reviews should not be a crisis response. They should be a weekly management habit.

Use T135-03 (Weekly Review Generation Tracker) to build a rhythm:

**Monday: Review audit.**
- How many new reviews last week? (Target: 5+)
- What is our current average rating?
- Any reviews awaiting response?

**Daily (2 minutes): Respond.**
- Check for new reviews every morning. Respond the same day.

**Friday: Generate.**
- Remind staff about the verbal ask for the weekend (busiest customer volume)
- Check QR codes are visible and undamaged
- Post a Google Business update for the weekend

**Monthly: Analyse.**
- What themes are appearing? (Positive: "great coffee", "friendly staff." Negative: "slow service", "limited food.")
- Are positive themes reflected in your marketing?
- Are negative themes being addressed operationally?

Set a target. If you currently get 2 reviews a week, aim for 5. If you get 5, aim for 8. More recent reviews carry more weight with Google's algorithm and with customers.

Celebrate milestones with the team. Reaching 200 reviews, hitting 4.5 stars, getting a review that specifically names a team member — these are wins worth acknowledging.
